posts.title: Cowboy on Bucking Bronco, 1910
posts.text:
A cowboy named Bud Eaton riding a bronco called Firefly at the Cheyenne Frontier Days rodeo, 1910. The horse is four feet off the ground. Eaton's hat is gone. His right hand is up and empty.

Cheyenne Frontier Days started in 1897 and hasn't stopped. By 1910 it was the biggest rodeo in the country. Railroad companies ran special excursion trains from Denver and Omaha. Tickets were fifty cents.

The cowboys who rode weren't performers. They were working ranch hands who entered competitions for prize money between cattle seasons. Eaton worked ranches across Wyoming and Montana the rest of the year.

Most of them made more money in a single rodeo weekend than in a month of ranch work.

Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division.

posts.title: Deadwood Stagecoach, 1889
posts.text:
The Deadwood stagecoach, 1889. Six passengers on top, driver holding the reins of a four-horse team, shotgun messenger riding beside him. Somewhere between Deadwood and Cheyenne, South Dakota Territory.

This route ran 300 miles through the Black Hills. It carried gold from Deadwood's mines to the railroad in Cheyenne. The coach was robbed so often that the company started sending armed guards in 1877.

The most famous holdup was by Sam Bass and his gang in September 1877. They got $350 in gold and the mail. The posse caught up with Bass nine months later in Round Rock, Texas.

By 1889 when this photo was taken, the railroad had reached Deadwood and the stagecoach was already nostalgia. They kept running it for tourists.

John C.H. Grabill, photographer. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Geronimo and Warriors, 1886
posts.text:
Geronimo and three of his Apache warriors, 1886. Taken during negotiations with General Crook in the Sierra Madre, just before Geronimo's final surrender.

Left to right: Yanozha (Geronimo's brother-in-law), Chappo (Geronimo's son), Fun (a warrior), and Geronimo himself, far right with the rifle across his knees.

Geronimo was about 57 years old here. He'd been fighting the U.S. and Mexican armies for 30 years. At this point his band was down to 38 people — 17 warriors, 14 women, 6 children, and Geronimo. Five thousand American soldiers were deployed to catch them.

He surrendered four months after this photo. Spent the last 23 years of his life as a prisoner of war at Fort Sill, Oklahoma. He sold autographed photos of himself for 25 cents each.

C.S. Fly, photographer. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Gold Miners at Sluice Box, Rockerville, Dakota Territory, 1889
posts.text:
Gold miners at a sluice box in Rockerville, Dakota Territory, 1889. Five men, ankle-deep in creek water, shoveling gravel into a wooden trough.

Rockerville was founded in 1876 during the Black Hills Gold Rush. Within two years it had 500 residents, a post office, and six saloons. By the time this photo was taken, most of the easy gold was gone and the miners were working harder for less.

The sluice box is simple technology. Running water carries gravel over ridges (called riffles). Heavy gold particles sink and catch on the ridges. Everything else washes through. A good day might yield a few dollars' worth of dust.

Today Rockerville has about 300 people. The saloons are still there.

John C.H. Grabill, photographer. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Canyon de Chelly, Arizona, 1873
posts.text:
Canyon de Chelly, Arizona Territory, 1873. Timothy O'Sullivan shot this on the Wheeler Survey — a government-funded mapping expedition that crossed some of the most remote country in North America.

The ruins in the alcove are White House Ruin, built by the Ancestral Puebloans around 1060 AD and abandoned by 1275. Eight hundred years old when O'Sullivan pointed his camera at them.

O'Sullivan hauled a darkroom in a modified ambulance wagon. Wet-plate photography. Each shot required coating a glass plate with collodion, exposing it while still wet, and developing it immediately. In the desert heat, the chemicals dried in minutes.

He was 33 years old on this expedition. He'd already photographed Gettysburg, the battle of Beaufort, and the silver mines of the Comstock Lode. He'd die of tuberculosis at 42.

Timothy O'Sullivan, Canyon de Chelly, 1873. Library of Congress.
